title: A Zero-Density Approach to Smooth Numbers
speaker: Adam Harper
abstract: A number is said to be $y$-smooth if all of its prime factors are less than $y$. Such numbers appear in many places throughout analytic and combinatorial number theory, and much work has been done to investigate their distribution.

proof_stages.label: Smooth-number framework and problem hierarchy
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines y-smooth numbers, introduces u = log x / log y, and sets up the sequence of problems: counting, arithmetic progressions, and Bombieri-Vinogradov-type averages, while flagging saddle points, Siegel zeros, and sparsity as the main phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Hildebrand-Tenenbaum saddle-point count
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting the prime number theorem's pole-of-zeta main term with the pole-free finite Euler product for smooth numbers, the speaker explains that shifting Perron's contour to the minimizing real point alpha gives the uniform asymptotic for Psi(x,y).

proof_stages.label: Individual arithmetic progressions and the Siegel-zero contrast
proof_stages.summary: Dirichlet characters reduce congruence counting to principal and nonprincipal character sums, and the smooth-number Euler product turns a possible Siegel zero into a small exponential contribution rather than the large obstruction familiar for primes.

proof_stages.label: Classical Bombieri-Vinogradov mechanisms for primes
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls how the prime Bombieri-Vinogradov theorem is attacked after passing to characters, either through Vaughan-type bilinear decompositions plus the large sieve or through explicit formulae and zero-density estimates.

proof_stages.label: Smooth-number Bombieri-Vinogradov theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: The new theorem is formulated as an average-over-q bound for the discrepancy between Psi(x,y;q,a) and the coprime-class main term Psi_q(x,y)/phi(q), beating the trivial Psi(x,y)-scale bound up to moduli just below the square-root barrier.

proof_stages.label: Sparsity obstruction to direct large-sieve methods
proof_stages.summary: The previous restriction on y is explained by the fact that the usual large sieve does not see the sparse support of smooth numbers and therefore loses too much once y is small.

proof_stages.label: Hybrid zero-density proof by conductor ranges
proof_stages.summary: The proof treats small conductor characters by individual equidistribution theorems, medium conductors by a log-free zero-density argument applied through the exponential relation between L(s,chi;y) and prime sums, and large conductors by the direct large sieve.

proof_stages.label: Effectivity and sparse-set consequences
proof_stages.summary: The final remarks emphasize that, unlike the prime case, the smooth-number theorem can be both effective and strong in suitable ranges because Siegel zeros are harmless and because the result applies to a very sparse sequence.

examples.name: The regime y = exp(sqrt(log x))
examples.note: This parameter choice makes u = sqrt(log x) and illustrates concretely that smooth numbers can have density about exp(-sqrt(log x)), much sparser than primes, rather than generating the method.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker plugs y=exp(sqrt(log x)) into u=log x/log y, gets u=sqrt(log x), and derives the resulting rough density e^{-sqrt(log x)}

examples.name: Two extremal large-sieve behaviours
examples.note: The cases of one very large character sum versus square-root cancellation in all sums illustrate the two terms in the large-sieve inequality without being part of the proof itself.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the two large-sieve extremes, relating one large character sum to the N-term and square-root cancellation across all sums to the Q-term

examples.name: Effective bound with a small fixed exponent parameter
examples.note: Choosing the theorem's parameter A as 1/2 or 1 in a large-u regime illustrates how the extra decaying smooth-number factor can make the result both effective and nontrivial.
